perturbation.original_text: This obligation of confidentiality and non- use does not apply to information which: 1. Was in our possession before the Inventor disclosed it to me/us. 2. Is made publicly available after its disclosure to me/us other than by any act or omission by us. 3. Becomes known to us after its disclosure by (b) (Inventor’s name) from a third party who is under no obligation of confidentiality to (b) (Inventor’s name)
perturbation.changed_text: This obligation of confidentiality and non-use does not apply to information which: 1. Was in our possession before the Inventor disclosed it to me/us. 2. Is made publicly available after its disclosure to me/us other than by any act or omission by us.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the third exception, the agreement now covers information rightfully obtained from a third party with no confidentiality obligation. This is problematic because it could unduly restrict the receiving party's ability to use information they legally obtained elsewhere, potentially violating fair competition and trade secret laws. The omission affects the scope of the confidentiality obligation, creating a legal risk.
perturbation.law_explanation: UTSA defines a trade secret as information that derives independent economic value from not being generally known or readily ascertainable by proper means. By not excluding information obtained from a third party without a confidentiality obligation, the agreement attempts to treat information as a trade secret even if it does not meet the UTSA definition, potentially leading to legal challenges.

perturbation.justification: The omission of the third exception in the changed text creates a contradiction. By removing the clause that excludes information rightfully obtained from a third party without confidentiality obligations, the modified agreement attempts to treat such information as confidential. The Law Explanation states this attempts to define trade secret even if it doesn't meet the UTSA definition, potentially leading to legal challenges. Though the snippets do not explicitly address this, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation, taken together, highlight that the modified text broadens the scope of confidentiality beyond what is legally sound, unduly restricting the receiving party's ability to use legally obtained information.

perturbation.original_text: I/We (Insert name of person or company to whom information is being disclosed) of (Address) hereby agree to keep confidential any information which has already or may be disclosed to us by: - (b) (Inventor’s name) of (c) (Address) Concerning the (d) (Insert brief description of invention) and we will not use it for our own benefit or disclose it to any other party without the written approval of: (b) (Inventor’s name)
perturbation.changed_text: I/We (Insert name of person or company to whom information is being disclosed) of (Address) hereby agree to keep confidential any information which has already or may be disclosed to us by: - (b) (Inventor’s name) of (c) (Address) Concerning the (d) (Insert brief description of invention)
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clause 'and we will not use it for our own benefit or disclose it to any other party without the written approval of: (b) (Inventor’s name)', the agreement no longer explicitly prohibits the receiving party from using the disclosed information for their own benefit or disclosing it to others. This weakens the core purpose of a non-disclosure agreement, potentially undermining trade secret protection and creating legal risk. The omission directly contradicts the intent of protecting the inventor's confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA defines 'misappropriation' to include disclosure or use of a trade secret without express or implied consent by a person who used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret, or who, at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that their knowledge of the trade secret was derived from a person who utilized improper means to acquire it, was acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use, or was derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use.  Removing the explicit prohibition against use and disclosure weakens the agreement's ability to prevent misappropriation as defined by CUTSA, because it is less clear that there is a duty to maintain the secrecy or limit the use.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The omission of the clause prohibiting the use of confidential information for personal benefit or disclosure to others directly contradicts the intent of trade secret protection laws. Snippet 1, referencing California Civil Code 3426.1, defines 'misappropriation' as the 'disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent'. By removing the explicit prohibition, the changed text weakens the agreement's ability to prevent misappropriation and undermines the 'duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use', as highlighted in snippet 1. The original text provided that protection, but the changed text does not, creating a significant contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: We (Insert name of person or company to whom information is being disclosed) of (Address) hereby agree to keep confidential any information which has already or may be disclosed to us by: - (b) (Inventor’s name) of (c) (Address) Concerning the (d) (Insert brief description of invention) and we will not use it for our own benefit or disclose it to any other party without the written approval of: (b) (Inventor’s name)
perturbation.changed_text: We (Insert name of person or company to whom information is being disclosed) of (Address) hereby agree to keep confidential any information which has already or may be disclosed to us by: - (b) (Inventor’s name) of (c) (Address) Concerning the (d) (Insert brief description of invention).
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause that requires written approval for use and disclosure effectively negates the entire NDA, as it allows the recipient to disclose and use the information as they see fit. This directly conflicts with the purpose of an NDA, which is to protect confidential information. Under the Defend Trade Secrets Act, an NDA helps establish reasonable measures to protect trade secrets, and this change eliminates a key protection measure.
perturbation.law_explanation: Under the DTSA, an owner of a trade secret can bring a civil action if the trade secret is misappropriated. The definition of misappropriation includes unauthorized disclosure or use of a trade secret. Removing the clause requiring written approval effectively removes the 'reasonable measures' to protect the trade secret, weakening a potential DTSA claim because there's no contractual basis to argue that unauthorized use or disclosure is a violation.

perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the requirement for written approval before disclosing confidential information, which contradicts the purpose of an NDA and weakens the protection of trade secrets. The perturbation explanation states that this change 'effectively negates the entire NDA'. The law explanation supports this by stating the removal 'weakening a potential DTSA claim'. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing 18 USC 1836, discusses civil proceedings for misappropriation of trade secrets. By removing the requirement for written approval, the modified contract makes it more difficult to prove unauthorized disclosure or use, thus contradicting the intent to protect trade secrets under the DTSA.

perturbation.original_text: This obligation of confidentiality and non- use does not apply to information which: 1. Was in our possession before the Inventor disclosed it to me/us. 2. Is made publicly available after its disclosure to me/us other than by any act or omission by us. 3. Becomes known to us after its disclosure by (b) (Inventor’s name) from a third party who is under no obligation of confidentiality to (b) (Inventor’s name) Accepted for and on behalf of (a) (Insert name of person or company to whom information is being disclosed)
perturbation.changed_text: This obligation of confidentiality and non- use does not apply to information which: 1. Was in our possession before the Inventor disclosed it to me/us. 2. Is made publicly available after its disclosure to me/us other than by any act or omission by us. Accepted for and on behalf of (a) (Insert name of person or company to whom information is being disclosed)
perturbation.explanation: By removing the exception for information received from a third party with no confidentiality obligation, the agreement could now cover information that is not truly a trade secret or is otherwise legitimately obtained. This overbroad restriction could be seen as an unreasonable restraint of trade, especially if the third party source is readily available.
perturbation.law_explanation: Comment b of Section 757 states that a trade secret must be secret and not generally known or readily ascertainable. If someone can obtain information from a third party without any obligation of confidentiality, it calls into question if the information can qualify as a trade secret. The removal of the exception broadens the scope of information the NDA would seek to protect to information that does not meet this qualification, increasing the likelihood it is deemed to be an invalid restriction and restraint of trade.
perturbation.justification: The omission of the clause regarding information received from a third party without confidentiality obligations directly contradicts the principle that a trade secret must not be generally known or readily ascertainable, as highlighted in the Law Explanation. While the Scraped Law Snippets are unhelpful, the Law Explanation clearly states that removing this exception broadens the scope of the NDA to include information that might not qualify as a trade secret. This creates an unreasonable restraint of trade because it attempts to protect information that the receiving party could legitimately obtain elsewhere.
